requirements.exclusion: A response to a notification carries no correlating ID, so it is indistinguishable on the wire from any other unsolicited response; unsolicited responses are flagged by base.result-id-matches and base.error-id-matches under BASE-004/BASE-009.
The umbrella clause the BASE and LIFE areas mechanize requirement-by-requirement; it has no observable beyond its constituents.
Dialect support is validation behavior inside each implementation; a trace shows schemas exchanged, not how the receiver processed them.
Whether validation happened, and against which dialect, is implementation-internal; validation outcomes need ground truth about inputs that a trace does not carry.
Judging graceful handling requires knowing the receiver's supported-dialect set, which is not in the trace.
Documentation lives outside the protocol session entirely.

Full dialect validation requires a JSON Schema engine, a dependency mcp-conformance-core deliberately excludes (serde-family only, 02-architecture.md); tools.input-schema-object (TOOL-003) enforces the structural floor.
What an implementation assumes is internal; only conduct that follows from an assumption could surface, and attributing it requires the assumption.
Whether a syntactically valid prefix is reverse-DNS for its author cannot be decided without knowing the author's domain.
Icon rendering support is client-internal capability with no wire footprint; the conditional premise (that the client renders icons) is equally invisible.
The umbrella for consumer-side icon hygiene (fetch policy, size limits, origin checks) — all consumer-internal conduct after the recorded session ends.
Rejection is client-side handling after receipt; the trace records the icon metadata served, not what the client did with it.

After negotiation, neither stdio nor HTTP message payloads carry a per-message protocol version, so respect is not observable in message traffic. The one wire artifact — the HTTP `MCP-Protocol-Version` header — is judged under TRAN-017 and TRAN-018.
The client's supported-version set is internal state with no wire footprint; the trace shows the version sent, not the versions available.
The server's supported-version set is internal state with no wire footprint; the trace shows the version answered, not the versions available.

Whether the client supports the answered version is client-internal; a continued session is only evidence of support if the premise is known. TRAN-018 warns on the adjacent observable (a mismatched MCP-Protocol-Version header).
Stdin closure, signals, and process exit are host-OS actions outside the trace event vocabulary (transport-open/close/abort record that a transport ended, not who ended it or how).
Checks may not consult time: trace timestamps are informational by design (determinism rule, 02-architecture.md), so timing conduct cannot be judged.
Checks may not consult time: trace timestamps are informational by design (determinism rule, 02-architecture.md), so timing conduct cannot be judged.
Checks may not consult time: trace timestamps are informational by design (determinism rule, 02-architecture.md), so timing conduct cannot be judged.

Framing is enforced at capture time: the JSON Lines trace format cannot represent an embedded-newline violation, so a parseable trace is evidence the rule held. Violations surface as capture-time parse failures, not as trace findings.

Judging Origin validation requires the server's configured allowlist, which recorded traces do not carry. Enforced instead as default-on policy in mcp-everything-server and exercised by its transport integration tests (roadmap M2).

A violation cannot be judged from a trace: trace documents are UTF-8 by construction, so recorded messages prove nothing was mis-encoded among what was recorded — and absence is not evidence, because capture tooling may drop undecodable bytes (mcp-everything-server's tap skips non-UTF-8 SSE chunks and unparseable POST bodies). No offline check is sound.

Recorded HTTP events carry status and conformance-relevant headers, not request lines, so endpoint topology is invisible in a trace. Enforced by mcp-everything-server's transport integration tests (roadmap M2).

Judging whether an Origin value is invalid requires the server's configured allowlist, which recorded traces do not carry (the TRAN-002 rationale). Enforced as default-on policy in mcp-everything-server with 403 tests (roadmap M2).

Socket binding is deployment state with no trace footprint. mcp-everything-server defaults to a loopback bind (the --bind clap default in src/main.rs), asserted by default_bind_is_loopback in tests/cli.rs.

Whether authentication is properly implemented is not decidable from one session's headers; authorization flows are their own scenario area (corpus/auth, post-M1) mirroring the official suite's `auth` suite.

Global uniqueness and entropy of an identifier cannot be established from the single sample a trace contains. mcp-everything-server's ids are pinned to the v4-UUID shape (OS RNG) and distinctness by session_ids_are_version_4_uuids_and_distinct in tests/http_policy.rs.
Secure handling (storage, exposure, logging discipline) is client-internal behavior with no wire footprint beyond the echo requirement judged under TRAN-013.
Whether a server requires a session ID is server configuration a trace does not carry; without it, a non-400 answer to a session-less request is not evidence either way.
Session termination is server-internal state; a trace cannot distinguish a terminated session (404 required) from a live one (404 forbidden).

The trace schema records HTTP request and response observations as independent events without request/response correlation, so 'in response to' cannot be established offline. Revisit when host-side capture lands (roadmap M3).
Which version a server assumed is internal state; subsequent traffic does not reveal it.

The server's supported-version set is not in the trace, so invalid-or-unsupported cannot be decided offline. mcp-everything-server answers an in-session request bearing an unsupported MCP-Protocol-Version with 400 (rmcp's streamable HTTP tower layer; the initialize exchange itself negotiates in-band and does not consult the header), asserted by unsupported_protocol_version_is_rejected_with_400 in tests/http_policy.rs.
How a client interprets stderr is internal, and stderr bytes are not part of the protocol trace (the trace records MCP messages and transport lifecycle, not diagnostics channels).
A client's supported-transport surface is a property of the implementation, not of any one session; a streamable-HTTP trace says nothing about whether stdio was available.
The trace vocabulary covers stdio and streamable HTTP; custom transports are outside the capture universe by construction (TransportKind is closed per revision).
Recorded HTTP events carry status and conformance-relevant headers, not request methods or targets, so this clause's subject is invisible in a trace.

The trace schema records HTTP request and response observations as independent events without request/response correlation, so which response answered which POST is not decidable offline; the official suite exercises this live and the conformance gate runs it.

Whether input was acceptable is server-internal judgment, and the trace schema records HTTP request and response observations as independent events without request/response correlation, so which response answered which POST is not decidable offline; the official suite exercises this live and the conformance gate runs it.
What a client could have handled is client-internal capability; a trace shows the case that occurred, not the cases supported.
SSE framing is consumed at capture: the tap reassembles `data:` lines into messages and drops event IDs and `retry` fields, so SSE-frame-level conduct is invisible in a trace.
SSE framing is consumed at capture: the tap reassembles `data:` lines into messages and drops event IDs and `retry` fields, so SSE-frame-level conduct is invisible in a trace.
Checks may not consult time: trace timestamps are informational by design (determinism rule, 02-architecture.md), so timing conduct cannot be judged.
Which HTTP stream delivered a message is not part of the trace vocabulary — events form a single capture-ordered sequence — so per-stream conduct cannot be judged from a recorded trace.

Relatedness is a semantic judgment over message content, and which HTTP stream delivered a message is not part of the trace vocabulary — events form a single capture-ordered sequence — so per-stream conduct cannot be judged from a recorded trace.
Which HTTP stream delivered a message is not part of the trace vocabulary — events form a single capture-ordered sequence — so per-stream conduct cannot be judged from a recorded trace.
How the server interpreted a disconnect is internal state; only conduct that follows from a misinterpretation could surface, and attributing it requires the interpretation.
A wish to cancel is client intent with no observable of its own; a session without a cancellation is not evidence one was wanted.

Unrelatedness is a semantic judgment over message content, and which HTTP stream delivered a message is not part of the trace vocabulary — events form a single capture-ordered sequence — so per-stream conduct cannot be judged from a recorded trace.
Which HTTP stream delivered a message is not part of the trace vocabulary — events form a single capture-ordered sequence — so per-stream conduct cannot be judged from a recorded trace.

Which HTTP stream delivered a message is not part of the trace vocabulary — events form a single capture-ordered sequence — so per-stream conduct cannot be judged from a recorded trace. A duplicated message in the capture order is also indistinguishable from a legitimately repeated notification.
SSE framing is consumed at capture: the tap reassembles `data:` lines into messages and drops event IDs and `retry` fields, so SSE-frame-level conduct is invisible in a trace.
SSE framing is consumed at capture: the tap reassembles `data:` lines into messages and drops event IDs and `retry` fields, so SSE-frame-level conduct is invisible in a trace.
Conditional on client intent (the wish to resume), and the request method is not captured; when a resumption does occur its `last-event-id` header is recorded, but absence proves nothing.
Which HTTP stream delivered a message is not part of the trace vocabulary — events form a single capture-ordered sequence — so per-stream conduct cannot be judged from a recorded trace.
Conditional on client intent (no longer needing the session), and the request method is not captured.

Restates TRAN-024's obligation as the section's first numbered step (two normative sentences, one constraint). Recorded HTTP events carry status and conformance-relevant headers, not request methods or targets, so the clause's subject is invisible in a trace.
The trigger condition — the tool list actually changing — is server-internal state a recorded trace cannot evidence, so absence of the notification is never proof of violation.
How a client weighs annotations is internal trust policy with no wire footprint.

Input validation, access control, rate limiting, and output sanitization are server-internal enforcement without a deterministic wire signature. Of the four duties, mcp-everything-server implements and tests input validation (tests/roundtrip.rs: tool_call_with_missing_required_argument_is_a_protocol_error and neighbors); access control beyond the transport-layer Host/Origin gate, rate limiting, and output sanitization are not in its scope — it serves fixed synthetic fixtures to a loopback-only harness.
Name-comparison semantics are consumer-internal; tools.name-unique (TOOL-008) applies case-sensitive comparison in accordance with this clause.
Client-side validation has no wire footprint; the server-side half of the contract is TOOL-011's check.
A host application's approval UX is outside the protocol session; a trace shows invocations that happened, not the approvals that gated them.
The trigger condition — the resource list actually changing — is server-internal state a recorded trace cannot evidence.
Whether the client can fetch a URI on its own is a property of the client's environment, not of the recorded session.

URI validation is server-internal enforcement; a trace shows the URIs exchanged, not whether the server vetted them. mcp-everything-server's read path is fail-closed — unknown or malformed URIs resolve to None — asserted by template_rejects_malformed_instantiations (src/resources.rs) and the unknown-URI protocol-error assertion in resources_surface_matches_the_scenarios (tests/roundtrip.rs).
The premise — whether the client could fetch the URI directly — is a property of the client's environment, not of the session (the RES-003 argument).
Whether a failure case actually occurred (the resource was absent, an internal fault happened) is server-side ground truth a trace does not carry.
Which resources are sensitive and what controls guard them is deployment policy with no per-session observable.
Permission checks are server-internal enforcement; a trace shows the operations served, not the checks that admitted them.
The trigger condition — the prompt list actually changing — is server-internal state a recorded trace cannot evidence.
Validation thoroughness is implementation-internal; a trace shows the prompt traffic, not the vetting applied to it.
Whether a failure case actually occurred (the name was invalid, an argument was missing) is server-side ground truth a trace does not carry.
Validation thoroughness is implementation-internal (the PROM-006 argument).
Client-side pagination handling is internal; a session whose lists fit one page exercises nothing.
Mechanized at the protocol level by LIFE-009's negotiated-capabilities-only check; this restatement adds no separately observable conduct.

Deciding whether free-form log data contains secrets or PII is semantic content analysis, not structural validation; offline heuristics would produce unsound, unfalsifiable verdicts — a fixed pattern list is deterministic but proves nothing about what it misses.
Whether a failure case actually occurred (the level was invalid for this server, configuration failed) is server-side ground truth a trace does not carry.
Rate policy, context relevance, naming consistency, and redaction are semantic internals (the LOG-002 argument).
Rate limiting, validation, access control, and monitoring are implementation-internal enforcement without trace-level observables.
Input validation, rate limiting, and access control are implementation-internal enforcement without a deterministic wire signature.
Whether a failure case actually occurred is server-side ground truth a trace does not carry.
Relevance ordering, fuzziness, rate policy, and input vetting are semantic internals without trace-level observables.
Debouncing and caching are client-internal and timing-shaped; checks may not consult time (determinism rule).
What a client assumes is internal state; the assumption has no wire footprint (MCP pagination requests carry no size parameter to inspect).
Whether a cursor is invalid is server-internal knowledge; a trace cannot distinguish a server accepting a stale-but-valid cursor from one silently tolerating an invalid one.
Cursor stability is cross-request server behavior over hidden state; gracefulness on invalid cursors requires knowing a cursor was invalid (the PAGE-003 argument).
How a client interprets a missing cursor, and what flows it supports, is client-internal; the wire-visible cursor discipline is PAGE-002's check.
